ui.bestiary.title: Bestiary
ui.bestiary.units.title: Units
ui.bestiary.noselection: Select a unit to view its bestiary entries.
ui.bestiary.unitinfo: A documented record of this creature’s nature, behavior, and traits.
ui.bestiary.entry.locked: Defeat this creature {0} more times to unlock this bestiary entry.
unit.zombie.bestiary.entry.1:
A common type of undead creature, most commonly found near the entrance and initial levels of the abyss.
They are slow and not particularly strong, but their numbers and persistence mark them a threat regardless.

They seem to be drawn to living creatures, attacking anything that moves within their vicinity with little provocation.
It also appears that their brain functions are severely limited or degraded, as they show no signs of strategy or tactics when attacking.
unit.zombie.bestiary.entry.2:
As one descends deeper, it appears that these zombies become less common, replaced by other, more dangerous creatures. It also appears that zombies further down are tougher and more resilient than those near the surface. What could be causing this change?

Is it simply that those further down have evolved to live in harsher conditions? Or is it something else at play.. Due to the natural state of decay these creatures appear to be in, and the state of our knowledge regarding the abyss itself, I can't say for certain.
unit.zombie.bestiary.entry.3:
There are more of them every time I descend.
As the abyss becomes more known and the number of explorers grows, so too will the numbers of these creatures.

With their close proximity to the surface, one might presume that one of the reasons the entrance became more regulated was to keep these creatures and others like them from escaping into the world above. However, would that really be a concern, given their slow and clumsy nature? Or is their ability to grow in numbers the real threat? One might presume the might of the surface world would be enough to deal with them, but what if they start to evolve further down the abyss...

Not much is known about their origin either. Could it be a curse, a disease, or something else entirely that causes them to rise from the dead? Did someone purposefully create them?
unit.elitezombie.bestiary.entry.1: Even tougher than regular zombies. It appears, however, that their strength remains relatively similar. Still, their increased resilience alone makes them a threat, especially in groups with regular zombies.
unit.knight.bestiary.entry.1:
At the surface, they 'protect the peace' and guard the kingdom from outside influences.
Down here, however, where their jurisdiction does not reach, they are little more than treasure hunters just like the rest of us. Their usually superior training and equipment, however, make them formidable opponents.
unit.knight.bestiary.entry.2: Many of these knights form groups, thinking it safer to work together. However, their rigid discipline and hierarchical structure often leads to infighting and power struggles when they realize that there is not much authority to be had down here in the abyss, compared to the surface.
unit.knight.bestiary.entry.3:
After the abyss was declared open territory, many knights, like everyone else seeking fortune, ventured into the depths; that much is obvious.
What is less obvious however is why so many of them continue to choose to do so?

One might think that the current situation at the surface would be enough. It is much safer to swindle and rob those returning from the abyss, so why choose to descend of their own will?
unit.eliteknight.bestiary.entry.1: They're stronger and better equipped than regular knights. Often they act as leaders for groups of regular knights. Their bulky armor makes them slow, but also very resilient.
unit.eliteknight.bestiary.entry.2:
Their armor and weapons remain well-maintained, far beyond what one should expect from life in the abyss.

On the surface, I've heard of whispers among other explorers that certain nobles fund expeditions in secret, hoping to claim exclusive rights to any treasures found deeper within. If this is true, these knights may be less 'treasure hunters' and more agents sent to secure these riches for their benefactors.
unit.copycat.bestiary.entry.1:
It appears to be some kind of mimic, copying not only my appearance, but also my fighting style.

This is troubling. If more of these creatures exist down here, they could pose a serious threat to explorers like myself. If they can copy us so perfectly, how are we to know who is real and who is not?
